papers.title: Decoding preeclampsia: A fusion of multi-view machine learning and multi-omics to identify putative inflammation-related mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Preeclampsia (PE) is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality worldwide, with placental inflammation recognized as a central pathogenic feature, yet the upstream triggers and inflammatory mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Here, we combined placental single-cell transcriptomics with gut metagenomic and metabolomic profiling to characterize inflammatory signatures in PE. Stratified analyses across clinical subgroups-defined by fetal number, onset timing, and fetal sex-revealed that placental single-cell transcriptomics coupled with multi-view machine learning consis
papers.summary: This study integrates placental single-cell transcriptomics with gut metagenomic and metabolomic data using multi-view machine learning to identify inflammatory mechanisms underlying preeclampsia. The analysis across clinical subgroups reveals consistent inflammatory signatures, offering new insights into the disease's pathogenesis.

papers.title: Mannheimia haemolytica strain-level diversity in cattle populations.
papers.abstract: High-resolution genomic characterization is essential for understanding diversity, pathogenicity, and transmission dynamics of bacterial pathogens. Mannheimia haemolytica (Mh) is the most consequential bacterial agent associated with bovine respiratory disease (BRD) in cattle, as a leading cause of morbidity, mortality, and antimicrobial use. Historically, BRD pathogens, including Mh, have been studied using culture or PCR approaches that provided limited ability to characterize fine-scale genomic variation across communities. Here, we evaluated target-enriched (TE) shotgun sequencing, a cultu
papers.summary: This study uses target-enriched shotgun sequencing to characterize the genomic diversity of Mannheimia haemolytica, a key bacterial pathogen in bovine respiratory disease, directly from cattle samples without the need for culture. The approach reveals fine-scale strain-level variation that was previously undetectable by traditional methods, providing new insights into pathogen transmission and diversity in cattle populations.

papers.title: Long-read metagenomics and methylation-based binning support the discovery of antibiotic resistance gene-host associations in complex communities.
papers.abstract: Antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) circulating among clinically relevant bacteria pose serious challenges to public health. Given the ancient and environmental bacterial origins of ARGs, a better understanding of the carriers of ARGs beyond the clinically most relevant species is urgently needed for longer-term resistance monitoring and intervention measures. While the risks of emerging ARGs from environmental sources have been recognized, the identification bottlenecks stem from the limitations of shotgun metagenomic sequencing and bioinformatic methods.
papers.summary: This study uses long-read metagenomics combined with methylation-based binning to link antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) to their bacterial hosts in complex microbial communities, overcoming limitations of short-read methods. By identifying ARG carriers beyond clinically relevant species, the work provides a clearer picture of resistance gene reservoirs in environmental and human-associated microbiomes. This approach improves the ability to monitor and predict the spread of antibiotic resistance.

papers.title: Effects of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor on the nasal microbial metagenome in cystic fibrosis.
papers.abstract: Mutation-specific cystic fibrosis (CF) transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulator therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has dramatically improved clinical outcomes for people with CF (pwCF), yet its impact on the nasal microbial metagenome remains insufficiently understood. This prospective, post-approval study investigated the impact of 15-week ETI therapy on sinonasal microbiota of pwCF aged 12 years and older. Whole-genome shotgun sequencing was performed on total DNA from 24 paired nasal lavage samples, with synthetic spike-in controls enabling absolute abundance no
papers.summary: This study shows that 15 weeks of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy alters the nasal microbial metagenome in people with cystic fibrosis, as measured by whole-genome shotgun sequencing of paired nasal lavage samples. The findings provide insight into how CFTR modulator therapy affects the sinonasal microbiome beyond clinical improvements.

papers.title: Protective role of lactate in allergic airway inflammation: mitigation of inflammatory injury, epithelial barrier integrity restoration, and gut-lung axis involvement.
papers.abstract: Allergic asthma is a prevalent respiratory disorder characterized by chronic airway inflammation and remodeling. Glycolysis has been reported to participate in pathogenesis of allergic asthma and increased lactate levels were found in asthma patients and mouse models. However, the function of lactate in allergic asthma remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study investigates the role of lactate in allergic asthma, finding that it can reduce airway inflammation, restore the integrity of the lung's epithelial barrier, and involve the gut-lung axis. The results suggest lactate may have a protective function in allergic airway inflammation, offering potential new therapeutic targets.

papers.title: Phage endolysin enables targeted manipulation of the small intestinal microbiota and uncovers niche overlap between oral and butyrate-producing taxa.
papers.abstract: Small intestinal oral bacterial overgrowth (SIOBO) is associated with impaired nutrient absorption and is a key contributor to environmental enteric disorder (EED) and stunted child growth. Here, we explore the use of a phage-derived lysin as a precision antimicrobial to selectively target Streptococcus salivarius within complex microbial communities. Using a newly developed bioinformatic and wet-lab pipeline, we identified, cloned, and produced a prophage-encoded lysin from S. salivarius and demonstrated its potent and specific lytic activity against 49 S. salivarius strains isolated from stu
papers.summary: This study developed a phage-derived lysin that specifically targets Streptococcus salivarius in the small intestine, enabling precise manipulation of the microbiota. Using this tool, the researchers discovered that removing S. salivarius allows butyrate-producing bacteria to expand, revealing niche competition between oral and beneficial gut microbes. This approach offers a potential therapeutic strategy for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth and related growth stunting in children.

papers.title: Complete genomes of 12 Streptococcus salivarius strains and one Streptococcus raffinosi strain isolated from the human oral cavity and gastric lavage.
papers.abstract: In this study, 11 Streptococcus salivarius strains, one Streptococcus raffinosi strain isolated from the human oral cavity and a gastric lavage sample, as well as one S. salivarius reference strain, were whole-genome sequenced to investigate their genomic heterogeneity. Bioinformatic analysis indicated two major clades.
papers.summary: This study presents complete genome sequences for 12 Streptococcus salivarius strains and one Streptococcus raffinosi strain from the human oral cavity and stomach, revealing two major genetic groups. The resource provides a foundation for understanding genomic diversity within these commensal bacteria.

papers.title: Blood mNGS: an effective non-invasive diagnostic tool for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.
papers.abstract: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) is a life-threatening opportunistic infection. Colonization is prevalent but cannot be reliably distinguished from active infection by conventional methods. Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) is a promising diagnostic tool, but the value of blood mNGS for diagnosis, microbial community comparison, and outcome-related associations in PJP remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that blood metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) is an effective non-invasive diagnostic tool for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), helping to distinguish active infection from colonization. The method shows promise for improving diagnosis and understanding microbial community associations with outcomes in this life-threatening infection.

papers.title: New Iflavirus identified in Chiroxiphia pareola birds from an ecotone area in northeast Brazil.
papers.abstract: Avian species play a key role in the ecology of viruses, acting as reservoirs, sentinels, and biological carriers across diverse environments. Here, we describe the detection and genomic characterization of a novel iflavirus identified in fecal samples from the Chiroxiphiapareola("blue-backed manakin") collected in areas of the Middle North region of Brazil, Maranhão state. Viral RNA was extracted from pool fecal samples and subjected to next-generation sequencing. De novo assembly and comparative analyses enabled the recovery of a complete picorna-like viral genome of 9,043 nucleotides, compr
papers.summary: Researchers discovered a new iflavirus in fecal samples from blue-backed manakin birds in Brazil, sequencing its complete genome. This highlights the role of wild birds as viral reservoirs and expands knowledge of iflavirus diversity.

papers.title: Gastric mucosal brushing enhances gastric microbiome profiling compared with conventional biopsy in gastric cancer.
papers.abstract: Gastric microbiota dysbiosis has been implicated in gastric carcinogenesis; however, the specific sampling method for assessing non-Helicobacter pylori microbiota associated with gastric cancer (GC) remains unestablished. This study compared gastric mucosal brushing with conventional biopsy for microbiota profiling in patients with GC and controls. We enrolled treatment-naïve GC patients and controls and analysed paired brushing and biopsy specimens using 16 S rRNA sequencing. Microbial analyses were compared between sampling methods and between the GC and control groups, and taxa-GC associati
papers.summary: This study shows that gastric mucosal brushing provides a more comprehensive and accurate profile of the stomach's microbiome compared to traditional biopsy, particularly for detecting non-Helicobacter pylori bacteria linked to gastric cancer. By comparing paired samples from cancer patients and controls using 16S rRNA sequencing, the authors found that brushing captures a richer and more diverse microbial community, potentially improving the identification of cancer-associated microbial signatures.

papers.title: The gut-sleep connection: a scoping review into microbiome alterations in sleep-wake and circadian disorders.
papers.abstract: Sleep is fundamental to brain, body, mental, and social health. In parallel, the gut microbiome is increasingly recognized as a key regulator of immune, metabolic, endocrine, and neurophysiological processes. This scoping review explored current evidence on gut microbiome alterations in relation to sleep duration and sleep loss, sleep-wake disorders, and circadian rhythm-related phenotypes. Searches of MEDLINE, Embase, and Cochrane were conducted up to February 2024. Of 2059 records identified, 54 studies met the eligibility criteria. Thirty-eight studies were observational, nine interventiona
papers.summary: This scoping review synthesizes evidence from 54 studies on how gut microbiome composition changes in relation to sleep duration, sleep loss, sleep-wake disorders, and circadian rhythm disruptions. The findings highlight a growing link between microbiome alterations and sleep health, though most studies are observational and more interventional research is needed.

papers.title: Post-wildfire soil bacterial MAGs and metagenome analysis.
papers.abstract: We compare the differences between bacteria in soil affected by a wildfire to an unaffected area from Minnewaska State Park, NY, located in the biodiverse northern Shawangunk Ridge. We detail our metagenomic sequencing data, relative abundance of bacterial phyla, and the taxonomic classification of three MAGs.
papers.summary: This study compares soil bacteria from a wildfire-affected area to an unaffected area in New York, presenting metagenomic sequencing data, bacterial phyla abundance, and three metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs). It provides a preliminary snapshot of how wildfire alters soil microbial communities.

papers.title: A complete Candidatus walczuchella monophlebidarum genome assembled from citrus leaf metagenomic sequences.
papers.abstract: We present the complete de novo assembly of a Candidatus Walczuchella monophlebidarum genome (286,606 bp), a flavobacterial endosymbiont of the giant-scale insect Icerya purchasi. The genome was assembled from metagenomic short read Illumina sequences obtained from DNA of citrus leaves collected in Carinthia, Austria in November 2024.
papers.summary: Researchers assembled the complete genome of a bacterial endosymbiont from DNA extracted from citrus leaves, revealing its genetic makeup. This genome provides insights into the symbiotic relationship between the bacterium and its insect host.

papers.title: Longitudinal shifts in oral microbiome composition and metabolic pathways associated with preterm birth.
papers.abstract: Oral dysbiosis in pregnant women with oral diseases has been associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes. However, the inter-individual variability in oral microbiome composition of pregnant women without any oral disease, and its role in preterm birth, has not been studied yet. Here, we have collected saliva from 20 term birth (TB) and 20 preterm birth (PTB) delivering women without any self-reported oral disease at three trimesters (n = 120). Microbial DNA was subjected to 16S rRNA gene sequencing for taxonomic classification, and microbial pathways were investigated by PICRUSt2. In a subset
papers.summary: This study examines how the oral microbiome changes during pregnancy in women without oral disease, comparing those who deliver preterm versus at term. By analyzing saliva samples across all three trimesters, the researchers found distinct shifts in microbial composition and metabolic pathways associated with preterm birth, suggesting that oral microbiome dynamics may serve as early indicators of pregnancy complications even in the absence of clinical oral disease.

papers.title: nf-core/magmap: Map metatranscriptomes to large collections of genomes.
papers.abstract: The lack of publicly available reference genomes has forced annotation of metatranscriptomes to either use direct alignment of sequence reads to reference databases or de novo assembly. As more and more natural environments are covered by metagenomic surveys, this is rapidly changing. This opens up the possibility of genome-resolved studies of prokaryotic metatranscriptomes by mapping to genomes from public repositories or metagenome-assembled genomes derived from the same environment. Here, we present the nf-core/magmap pipeline that provides a reproducible, easy-to-access, and well-documente
papers.summary: This paper introduces nf-core/magmap, a reproducible pipeline for mapping metatranscriptomic reads to large collections of prokaryotic genomes, enabling genome-resolved studies of gene expression in environmental samples. It addresses the challenge of analyzing metatranscriptomes without complete reference genomes by leveraging publicly available genomes and metagenome-assembled genomes.

papers.title: Characterizing the composition and diversity of the vaginal microbiome in ovarian cancer.
papers.abstract: Vaginal dysbiosis, characterized by Lactobacillus depletion and anaerobic enrichment, may be relevant to ovarian cancer (OC) outcomes, yet comprehensive microbiome characterization in OC patients remains limited. Here, we characterize the prevalence and predictors of vaginal microbiome dysbiosis among racially diverse OC patients in the US.
papers.summary: This study characterizes the vaginal microbiome in a racially diverse group of ovarian cancer patients, finding that dysbiosis (low Lactobacillus, high anaerobic bacteria) is common. The findings suggest that vaginal microbiome composition may be relevant to ovarian cancer outcomes and could inform future research on prognosis or treatment.

papers.title: Quartet: Disentangling positive and negative components of microbial interactions.
papers.abstract: Interspecies interactions are characterized conventionally by the net influence, positive or negative, a species exerts on another. Community ecology theories rely on these net interactions to describe the behaviour of multispecies communities. The net interactions in turn comprise positive and negative components, arising typically from cross-feeding metabolites and competition for resources. The components remain challenging to disentangle, compromising descriptions of community behaviour. Here, we devised a method to estimate the components when metabolic interactions predominate. We concei
papers.summary: This paper introduces a method called Quartet that separates microbial interactions into positive (e.g., cross-feeding) and negative (e.g., resource competition) components, rather than just measuring net effects. This disentanglement improves the accuracy of predicting community behavior in metabolic-driven microbial systems.

papers.title: RiboZAP: a species-agnostic pipeline for rRNA depletion probe design in metatranscriptomics.
papers.abstract: Metatranscriptomic (MetaT) sequencing provides insights into gene expression and functional activity within microbial communities, but its utility is limited by the high abundance of ribosomal RNA (rRNA), which often accounts for ≥ 90% of total RNA. Efficient rRNA depletion is therefore essential to maximize mRNA coverage and sequencing efficiency. Commercial rRNA depletion kits can effectively reduce rRNA content; they are typically optimized for specific host microbiomes and often underperform in others. For example, probes designed for the human gut microbiome frequently show reduced effici
papers.summary: RiboZAP is a new computational pipeline that designs species-agnostic rRNA depletion probes for metatranscriptomics, overcoming the limitations of commercial kits that are often optimized for specific microbiomes. The tool enables efficient rRNA removal across diverse microbial communities, improving mRNA coverage and sequencing efficiency.

papers.title: Interleukin-8 in health and disease.
papers.abstract: Cytokines are among the main factors involved in indirect cell-to-cell communication in physiological and pathological conditions. In this way, cytokines are soluble and tissue-resident easily detectable biomarkers for the diagnosis and the prediction of clinical outcome, as well as drug targets. Over the years, the pleiotropic IL-8 gained a central role in the pathogenesis of several immunological disorders, spanning from autoimmune pathologies and sepsis to neurological, liver and cardiovascular diseases. In several solid cancers, IL-8 not only serves as a prognostic biomarker but also as pr
papers.summary: This review highlights the central role of the cytokine interleukin-8 (IL-8) in a wide range of diseases, including autoimmune disorders, sepsis, neurological conditions, and cancer. It emphasizes IL-8's utility as a biomarker for diagnosis and prognosis, as well as its potential as a drug target across multiple pathological conditions.

papers.title: Frequent Sweetened Beverage Consumption Is Associated With Accelerated Biological Aging: Evidence From a Population-Based Study and Gut Microbiota Analysis.
papers.abstract: Although substantial evidence links sweetened beverage (SB) intake to chronic diseases, its association with biological aging and underlying mechanisms remains unclear. This population-based study included 9104 adults aged 18-80 years from the WELL-China cohort. SB consumption was assessed using a validated food frequency questionnaire. Biological age and biological age acceleration (BAacc) were estimated using the Klemera and Doubal method (KDM). After multivariable adjustment, frequent SB consumption was significantly associated with accelerated KDM-derived BAacc, compared with non-consumers
papers.summary: Frequent consumption of sweetened beverages is linked to accelerated biological aging, as measured by a validated aging clock, in a large Chinese adult cohort. The study also suggests that changes in gut microbiota composition may partly mediate this association.

papers.title: UniCoracle: Automated hierarchical Feature Selection via Bottom-Up Propagation and Top-Down Skimming using the UniCorP algorithm and the Coracle machine-learning framework.
papers.abstract: Identifying meaningful associations between microbial communities and measured physiological or environmental variables becomes increasingly complex and computationally demanding given the continuous growth of microbiome datasets. The Coracle machine learning (ML) framework was recently developed to address this by integrating multiple data transformations, feature selection techniques, and ML models to yield condensed lists of features that align to target variables of interest. Further, we recently developed the UniCorP feature aggregation algorithm to identify uniquely correlated features (
papers.summary: UniCoracle is a new method that combines the UniCorP algorithm with the Coracle machine learning framework to efficiently identify key microbial features linked to health or environmental outcomes. It uses a hierarchical approach to filter through large microbiome datasets, reducing computational demands while improving the accuracy of feature selection. This tool helps researchers find meaningful biological associations in complex microbial communities.

papers.title: CAMBRA caries risk stratification is associated with distinct salivary and supragingival plaque microbiomes in pre-orthodontic patients.
papers.abstract: Fixed orthodontic appliances impair oral hygiene and increase the risk of dental caries and white spot lesions (WSLs). Although the Caries Management by Risk Assessment (CAMBRA) tool enables multifactorial caries risk evaluation, its association with the oral microbiome remains unclear. In this cross-sectional study, we examined the relationship between CAMBRA risk classification, clinical indices, and salivary and supragingival plaque microbiomes in 149 pre-orthodontic patients. Participants were classified into Low, Moderate, High, and Extreme risk groups based on CAMBRA. We evaluated the de
papers.summary: This study found that CAMBRA caries risk categories in pre-orthodontic patients are associated with distinct microbial communities in saliva and supragingival plaque, suggesting that the oral microbiome varies with caries risk. The findings indicate that CAMBRA risk stratification reflects underlying microbial differences, which could inform personalized preventive strategies.

papers.title: The Role of Gut Microbiota in the Association between Air Pollution and Cognitive Function in Older Adults.
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: Growing evidence links air pollution to cognitive dysfunction in older adults. The gut microbiome and circulating metabolites present an important yet unexplored pathway given their crucial role in the gut-brain axis. OBJECTIVES: We aimed to explore the potential roles of gut bacteria, fungi, microbial functional potentials, and circulating metabolites in the association of residential PM2.5 and O3 exposure with cognitive dysfunction. METHODS: We analyzed gut microbiome data from 1,027 older adults using metagenome and internal transcribed spacer sequencing to profile bacterial and
papers.summary: This study investigates whether gut microbiota and circulating metabolites mediate the link between air pollution (PM2.5 and O3) and cognitive decline in older adults. Using metagenomic and fungal sequencing data from over 1,000 participants, the authors aim to identify microbial and metabolic pathways that could explain how air pollution affects brain health through the gut-brain axis.

papers.title: Association between salivary microbiota-related amino acid metabolic dysregulation and tacrolimus-induced gingival overgrowth following kidney transplantation.
papers.abstract: Kidney transplant (KT) recipients require lifelong immunosuppressive therapy to prevent allograft rejection. Drug-induced gingival overgrowth (DIGO) is a notable adverse effect of tacrolimus, for which effective preventive or therapeutic strategies are lacking. Dysbiosis of the oral microbiota has been implicated as a major risk factor for DIGO. However, its mechanistic role remains poorly understood.
papers.summary: This study investigates the link between oral microbiota dysbiosis and tacrolimus-induced gingival overgrowth in kidney transplant recipients. The findings suggest that amino acid metabolic dysregulation in the salivary microbiome may contribute to this adverse effect, offering potential targets for prevention or treatment.

papers.title: Pre-treatment Gut Microbiome Diversity and Function Linked to Cytotoxic and Natural Killer Cell Immune Responses after N-803 Treatment in People with HIV.
papers.abstract: N-803, an IL-15 superagonist, is currently being studied in clinical trials as a treatment to reverse HIV latency. However, its effects on the gut microbiome are not well understood.
papers.journal: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America
papers.summary: This study investigates how the gut microbiome influences immune responses to N-803, an experimental HIV treatment. The researchers found that pre-treatment gut microbiome diversity and function are linked to cytotoxic and natural killer cell immune responses after N-803 treatment in people with HIV. This suggests that the gut microbiome may play a role in modulating the effectiveness of this therapy.

papers.title: Grassland afforestation more than forestry intensification shapes soil multifunctionality via microbial compositional change under abiotic constraints.
papers.abstract: Soil ecosystem multifunctionality (EMF) is driven by the interplay of abiotic and biological factors, yet how these interactions respond to anthropogenic pressures remains poorly understood. Here, we evaluated how grassland afforestation and its intensification shape soil edaphic conditions, microbial diversity, and EMF along a 200 km grassland-eucalypt plantation transect in Argentina. EMF was estimated, accounting for six ecosystem functions related to nutrient provisioning, organic matter cycling, and pathogen control. Microbial diversity was studied through the taxonomic, functional, and p
papers.summary: This study examines how converting grasslands to eucalyptus plantations (afforestation) versus intensifying existing forestry affects soil health in Argentina. The researchers found that afforestation had a stronger negative impact on soil multifunctionality than forestry intensification, primarily by altering microbial communities under water-limited conditions. The findings highlight that land-use change, rather than management intensity, poses a greater risk to soil ecosystem functions.

papers.title: Fermentative iron reduction by a psychrotolerant Clostridium-dominant consortium enriched from Antarctic penguin-impacted soils.
papers.abstract: Microbial iron cycling regulates nutrient availability and redox balance in global ecosystems, yet its pathways remain underexplored in ice-free Antarctic terrestrial ecosystems. This study reports the enrichment of a psychrotolerant microbial consortium from penguin-impacted soils on Beaufort Island, Antarctica, capable of reducing Fe(III) to Fe(II) at 4 °C via an anaerobic (likely fermentative) iron-reducing pathway. The consortium was dominated by Clostridium sensu stricto 13 and completely reduced 230 mg L-1 Fe(III) citrate within three months and drove the biogenic formation of magnetite
papers.summary: Researchers enriched a cold-tolerant microbial community from Antarctic penguin-impacted soils that reduces iron at 4°C, likely through a fermentative pathway. The consortium, dominated by Clostridium bacteria, completely converted iron within three months and produced the mineral magnetite. This reveals a previously underappreciated microbial iron cycle in Antarctic terrestrial ecosystems.

papers.title: Bio-stimulants improve tomato growth by regulating the rhizosphere microbiome involved in phosphorus and nitrogen cycling.
papers.abstract: Bio-stimulants are promising environment friendly alternatives to support sustainable agricultural development, capable of boosting crop growth and yield while cutting down excessive dependence on chemical synthetic fertilizers. Nevertheless, the explicit regulatory mechanisms by which bio-stimulants exert the role of growth-promoting functions still remain largely unclear and require further systematic clarification. In this study, we explored the influences of bio-stimulants (rich in humic acid) on tomato growth performance and rhizosphere microbial community assembly via greenhouse trials,
papers.summary: This study shows that bio-stimulants rich in humic acid improve tomato growth by altering the rhizosphere microbiome to enhance phosphorus and nitrogen cycling. The findings provide mechanistic insight into how these environmentally friendly alternatives to synthetic fertilizers promote crop growth.

papers.title: Effects of Non-Surgical Periodontal Therapy on Dental Plaque Microbiome.
papers.abstract: Periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 40% of U.S. adults aged 30 years and older, is characterized by dysbiosis of the dental plaque microbiome. However, although scaling and root planing (SRP) is the cornerstone of periodontal treatment, its effects on the taxonomic composition and functional potential of the dental plaque microbiome remain incompletely understood. In this study, we used whole-metagenome shotgun sequencing to characterize taxonomic composition and functional potential in dental plaque microbiomes collected from 39 patients with Stage II or III
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: This study uses whole-metagenome shotgun sequencing to characterize how scaling and root planing (SRP) alters the dental plaque microbiome in patients with periodontitis. The findings provide detailed insights into taxonomic and functional changes following non-surgical periodontal therapy.

papers.title: Metagenomic investigation of VIM-type metallo-β-lactamase-producing multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa associated with a hospital outbreak across multiple hospital units in Saudi Arabia.
papers.abstract: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) represent a public health challenge, particularly when associated with VIM-type metallo-β-lactamases (MBLs), which limit therapeutic options. Conventional microbiological methods may underestimate resistance determinants and transmission dynamics. Long-read metagenomic sequencing is a promising approach for Pathogen detection, resistome characterization, and genomic surveillance.
papers.journal: Journal of infection and public health
papers.summary: This study used long-read metagenomic sequencing to investigate a hospital outbreak of multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa carrying VIM-type metallo-β-lactamases across multiple units in Saudi Arabia. The approach revealed resistance determinants and transmission dynamics that conventional methods might miss, highlighting the utility of metagenomics for genomic surveillance of hospital outbreaks.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics and predictive precision systems for poultry meat and egg quality: Mechanisms, applications, and commercial challenges.
papers.abstract: Poultry meat and egg quality result from complex interactions among host genetics, metabolism, nutrition, microbiome ecology, physiology, management practices, and environmental conditions. These multidimensional interactions limit the predictive capacity of conventional phenotype-based approaches and increasingly necessitate systems-level frameworks capable of capturing biological complexity. Recent advances in multi-omics technologies have transformed poultry quality research by enabling integrated analyses of genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, lipidomic, epigenomic, and microb
papers.summary: This review discusses how multi-omics technologies (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, etc.) can be integrated to better understand and predict poultry meat and egg quality, which is influenced by complex interactions between genetics, nutrition, microbiome, and environment. It highlights the limitations of traditional phenotype-based methods and advocates for systems-level frameworks to capture biological complexity, while also addressing commercial challenges in applying these approaches.

papers.title: Phylogenetically diverse Mucorales-Mycetohabitans endosymbiotic interactions identified from whole-genome sequencing using a targeted metagenomic assembly pipeline.
papers.abstract: Endosymbiotic bacteria of the genus Mycetohabitans are obligate intracellular associates of Mucorales fungi, yet the understanding of their diversity, distribution and evolutionary dynamics is in its infancy. By screening 1,696 public sequencing datasets from Mucorales fungi, we detected Mycetohabitans in 46 fungal accessions spanning 5 host taxa across the fungal genera Rhizopus and Apophysomyces. These included 13 previously unreported associations. Genome reconstruction yielded 38 Mycetohabitans metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs), of which 34 were of high quality. Incorporating these MAGs
papers.summary: This study identifies new endosymbiotic relationships between Mycetohabitans bacteria and Mucorales fungi by analyzing public sequencing data, uncovering 13 previously unknown associations and reconstructing 38 bacterial genomes. The findings expand our understanding of the diversity and evolutionary dynamics of these obligate intracellular symbionts.

papers.title: Phylogenetic Authentication of Amplicon Sequence Variants in Single-Specimen Metabarcoding of Tropical Insects.
papers.abstract: High-throughput sequencing (HTS) allows large-scale DNA barcoding of individually tagged specimens ('megabarcoding'), but deep amplicon sequencing produces a mixture of authentic mitochondrial sequences together with nuclear pseudogenes (NUMTs), environmental and cross-sample contaminants, and sequencing artefacts. Standard approaches relying on read clustering or dominant-read selection often fail to classify these types, leading to incorrect taxonomic identifications and species counts. We developed an authentication framework by integrating abundance filtering, phylogenetic placement and ta
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational framework that uses phylogenetic placement and abundance filtering to distinguish authentic mitochondrial DNA sequences from contaminants and errors in high-throughput insect barcoding data, improving the accuracy of species identification.

papers.title: Multi-cohort evidence for impaired microbial support of the methionine cycle in children with autism spectrum disorder.
papers.abstract: The contribution of gut microbiota to outcomes of autism spectrum disorders (ASD) has been increasingly appreciated in recent years. With the accumulating evidence on ASD-driven alterations of the gut microbiota, heterogeneities arise across different reports. To account for variabilities in gut microbiota, clinical representations of ASD and data processing approaches, as well as limitations in sample sizes among the existing gut microbiota studies for ASD, the present multi-cohort analysis applied a standard bioinformatic and statistical pipeline on the publicly available gut metagenomic seq
papers.summary: This study analyzed multiple publicly available gut metagenomic datasets and found consistent evidence that children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) have impaired microbial support for the methionine cycle, a key metabolic pathway. The findings suggest a potential mechanistic link between gut microbiota alterations and ASD, helping to reconcile some of the variability seen in previous individual studies.

papers.title: VirBinn improves viral genome binning from metagenomic Hi-C through graph diffusion.
papers.abstract: Metagenomic Hi-C provides in situ proximity signals that can improve genome binning and enable virus-host-association analysis. However, viral genome recovery remains difficult because virus-virus Hi-C contact matrices are extremely sparse. Viral genomes are small, often low-abundance, and frequently assemble into short contigs, leaving many true within-genome links unobserved and causing viral bins to fragment.
papers.summary: VirBinn is a new computational tool that improves the recovery of viral genomes from metagenomic Hi-C data by using graph diffusion to address the problem of sparse contact signals. This method helps to better assemble viral genomes and link them to their hosts, overcoming challenges posed by the small size and low abundance of viral sequences.

papers.title: Decoding Viral Dark Matter: Metagenomic Prokaryotic Virus Characterization With Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny.
papers.abstract: Viral metagenomics is an increasingly powerful tool for understanding the function and structure of viruses across the diverse environments of our planet. However, decoding the functional potential of prokaryotic viral metagenomes is extremely challenging. Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny are complementary open-source prokaryotic viral genome annotation tools that utilize a variety of bioinformatics approaches to maximally annotate viral metagenomes. This article describes a protocol for installing and running these tools on a viral metagenomic dataset, followed by visualization of annotations us
papers.summary: This protocol describes how to use three complementary open-source tools (Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny) to annotate prokaryotic viral genomes from metagenomic data, addressing the challenge of decoding 'viral dark matter'. The tools employ different bioinformatics approaches to maximize functional annotation, and the protocol includes steps for installation, running the tools, and visualizing the results.

papers.title: Quantitative Profiling and Metabolic Study of Caffeoylquinic Acids and Their Metabolites by Liquid Chromatography-Tandem Mass Spectrometry in Rats and Laying Hens.
papers.abstract: Caffeoylquinic acids are natural polyphenolic compounds with notable antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. This study developed and validated a sensitive high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry method for the simultaneous quantification of isochlorogenic acid A (ICQA) and 22 metabolites in serum. The method exhibited excellent sensitivity with limits of quantification of 0.625-1.25 µg/L (signal-to-noise ratio = 10), inter/intra-day precision below 15%, and comprehensive coverage of phase I/II and microbial metabolites. Application of the method in rats and layin
papers.summary: This study developed a sensitive liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry method to simultaneously quantify isochlorogenic acid A and 22 of its metabolites in serum, achieving high sensitivity and precision. The method was successfully applied in rats and laying hens, enabling detailed metabolic profiling of these antioxidant compounds.

papers.title: Mapping potential pathogen profiling in cetacean blow: comparative insights from sequencing technologies.
papers.abstract: Cetaceans play a critical role in marine ecosystems and function as sentinel species for detecting environmental perturbations, underscoring the importance of assessing their health for effective marine conservation. This study employed 16S rRNA gene sequencing to characterize the prokaryotic communities present in exhaled breath condensate (EBC) samples from cetaceans, utilizing both short-read (Illumina) and long-read (PacBio) sequencing platforms. Putative pathogenic taxa were identified using the Multiple Bacterial Pathogen Detection (MBPD) database. Substantial differences in microbial co
papers.summary: This study used 16S rRNA gene sequencing on both short-read (Illumina) and long-read (PacBio) platforms to characterize microbial communities in cetacean exhaled breath condensate, identifying potential pathogens via a specialized database. The findings reveal significant differences in microbial composition between sequencing technologies, highlighting the importance of platform choice for accurate pathogen profiling in marine mammal health monitoring.

papers.title: Gut microbiomes of tribal communities in India vary with dairy and grain consumption.
papers.abstract: Highly diverse gut microbiomes of non-industrialized populations share similarities with ancestral states of symbiosis and are linked to low rates of chronic inflammatory diseases. Yet there is still limited understanding of the diverse array of non-industrialized gut microbiomes throughout the world, including among the tribal populations of India. In this study, we surveyed dietary and fecal microbiome variation among 76 adults from eight tribal communities in four biogeographic regions of India, including Warli on the western coast, Gond and Madia in the northeast Deccan Plateau, Kabui (or
papers.summary: This study surveyed the gut microbiomes and diets of 76 adults from eight tribal communities across India, finding that dietary patterns, particularly dairy and grain consumption, are associated with variations in gut microbiome composition. The research highlights the diversity of non-industrialized gut microbiomes and their links to traditional diets, which may inform understanding of chronic disease patterns.

papers.title: Human gut flagellome profiling using FlaPro reveals TLR5-related phenotype-specific alterations in IBD.
papers.abstract: Flagellin, the structural protein of bacterial flagella, activates the innate immune receptor Toll-like receptor 5 (TLR5). However, the ability of different flagellins to bind and stimulate TLR5 varies widely, suggesting that the composition of an individual's flagellin repertoire, defined as flagellome, may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation. Here, we developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline for quantification and functional annotation of human gut flagellomes. Functional categories in FlaPro are derived from a machine learning model trained on experimentally characteri
papers.summary: The authors developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline to quantify and functionally annotate flagellin proteins (the flagellome) in the human gut microbiome. Using this tool, they found that the composition of flagellins, which activate the immune receptor TLR5, is altered in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients in a phenotype-specific manner. This suggests that individual differences in the flagellome may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation.

papers.title: The effect of different milk pretreatment methods on microbiome community development during Herrgårds cheese production and ripening.
papers.abstract: One of the biggest challenges for dairy producers is the substantial variability in final product properties caused by changes in the production environment. In cheese production, this variation is influenced by several factors, particularly the milk base and its pretreatment, which shape the microbiome throughout the process and ultimately affect the cheese's organoleptic characteristics. To examine the impact of three different pretreatments for pasteurised milk- microfiltration, protein fortification, and pasteurisation only (control)- on microbiome dynamics, we generated metagenome sequenc
papers.summary: This study investigates how different milk pretreatments (microfiltration, protein fortification, and pasteurization alone) affect the microbiome development during Herrgårds cheese production and ripening. Using metagenome sequencing, the authors found that pretreatment methods significantly shape the microbial community dynamics, which in turn influence cheese properties. The findings highlight the importance of milk processing in controlling fermentation outcomes.

papers.title: Depletion of Blautia wexlerae and Parabacteroides distasonis in adiposity-related prehypertension.
papers.abstract: Prehypertension is more likely to develop into hypertension in individuals with adiposity. We aimed to explore how adiposity influences prehypertension through gut microbiota.
papers.summary: This study found that individuals with adiposity-related prehypertension have lower levels of two specific gut bacteria, Blautia wexlerae and Parabacteroides distasonis, suggesting these microbes may play a protective role against blood pressure elevation in obesity. The findings highlight a potential link between gut microbiome composition and the progression from prehypertension to hypertension in people with excess body fat.

papers.title: Metagenomic analysis of tongue samples from healthy subjects identifies distinct microbiome orotypes.
papers.abstract: The tongue dorsum harbors a complex microbiome that remains incompletely characterized.
papers.summary: This study uses metagenomic analysis of tongue samples from healthy individuals to identify distinct microbiome community types, or 'orotypes,' revealing previously unrecognized patterns in the oral microbiome composition. The findings provide a more detailed characterization of the tongue microbiome, which may have implications for understanding oral health and disease.

papers.title: A two-hit ecological framework linking social context to caries-associated microbiome shifts in children.
papers.abstract: Dental caries arises from an ecological imbalance within a complex community. How chronic social context relates to ecological heterogeneity and dysbiosis-associated microbial shifts in school-age children remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study proposes a 'two-hit' ecological framework linking chronic social context to microbiome shifts associated with dental caries in children. It finds that social factors contribute to ecological heterogeneity and dysbiosis, offering a new perspective on how environment shapes oral microbial communities.

papers.title: Multi-omics reveal soil microbial dysbiosis and metabolite toxicity as drivers of blueberry continuous cropping obstacles.
papers.abstract: Blueberry (Vaccinium spp.) are one of the most economically important fruit trees globally. However, due to continuous cropping have limited the industry's ability to produce consistently over the long term, and the mechanism underlying the development of this continuous cropping problem is not yet fully understood. In this study, we applied metagenomic and metabolomic to systematically detect changes in microbial community structure, function and metabolic profiles in rhizosphere and non-rhizosphere soils after different years of continuous blueberry cultivation (0, 2, 4, and 6 years) in Dali
papers.summary: This study uses metagenomics and metabolomics to investigate how continuous blueberry cultivation alters soil microbial communities and metabolite profiles, finding that microbial dysbiosis and toxic metabolite accumulation drive the 'continuous cropping obstacle' that reduces long-term yield. The research provides mechanistic insights into how years of replanting the same crop degrade soil health.

papers.title: A targeted PCR approach for the detection of IOLA in canine infectious respiratory disease samples during an atypical CIRD outbreak in winter 2023.
papers.abstract: During winter 2023, an atypical canine infectious respiratory disease (aCIRD) outbreak was associated with high case-fatality rates and poor antibiotic response. Preliminary metagenomics investigations claimed partial sequences resembling Infectious Organism Lurking in Human Airways (IOLA), a poorly characterized bacterium first described in humans, in canine respiratory samples. However, its detectability remained uncertain and required systematic molecular investigation.
papers.summary: This study developed a targeted PCR method to detect the bacterium IOLA in dogs during a severe respiratory disease outbreak in winter 2023. The researchers confirmed that IOLA, previously only known in humans, was present in canine samples, providing a new diagnostic tool for investigating this atypical outbreak.

papers.title: Moisture-mediated resource availability shapes rhizosphere and bulk soil microbial structure and function post-rainfall.
papers.abstract: Rainfall pulses drive rapid ecological changes in alpine grasslands, but their compartment-specific effects on short-term soil microbial dynamics remain unclear.
papers.summary: This study shows that after rainfall, moisture-driven resource availability rapidly alters the structure and function of microbial communities in both the rhizosphere (near plant roots) and bulk soil of alpine grasslands, with distinct responses in each compartment. The findings highlight how short-term water pulses can shape soil microbial ecology, which is important for understanding nutrient cycling in these sensitive ecosystems.

papers.title: Traditional Chinese medicine as a potential barrier-oriented sensitization strategy for immune checkpoint blockade in microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer: from resistance mechanisms to translational validation.
papers.abstract: Microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer (MSS-CRC) is refractory to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), mainly because of low tumor immunogenicity, impaired antigen presentation, immune exclusion, and myeloid- or Treg-dominated immunosuppression. Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), characterized by multi-component and multi-target regulation, may provide complementary approaches for modulating these resistance barriers. This review summarizes the rationale, evidence, and translational requirements for integrating TCM with ICI-based therapy in MSS-CRC.
papers.summary: This review explores how traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) could help overcome resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors in microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer (MSS-CRC), which is typically unresponsive to immunotherapy. The authors propose that TCM's multi-target effects may address key barriers like low tumor immunogenicity and immune suppression, and they summarize the evidence and translational needs for combining TCM with immunotherapy.

papers.title: Investigating the Gut Microbiota-Inflammatory Cytokine-Skin Axis in Inflammatory Skin Diseases: Evidence from Mendelian Randomization.
papers.abstract: Previous studies have reported causal associations between gut microbiota and inflammatory skin diseases; however, the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Inflammatory cytokines may mediate the gut-skin axis. This study aimed to investigate the mediating roles of inflammatory cytokines in the associations between gut microbiota and inflammatory skin diseases.
papers.summary: This study uses Mendelian randomization to explore how gut bacteria influence inflammatory skin diseases through inflammatory cytokines. The findings suggest that certain gut microbes affect skin inflammation via specific cytokines, providing evidence for a gut-skin axis. This work helps clarify the biological pathways linking the gut microbiome to skin health.

papers.title: Shuyu Wan Potentiates PD-1 Inhibitor Efficacy in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Integrated Bioinformatics and Experimental Evidence for Gut Microbiota-Tumor Immune Crosstalk.
papers.abstract: Therapeutic heterogeneity limits the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Shuyu Wan (SYW), a classic TCM formula, has shown potential in modulating gut microbiota (GM) and enhancing immunotherapy, yet its synergistic mechanism with PD-1 inhibitors remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study shows that the traditional Chinese medicine formula Shuyu Wan enhances the effectiveness of PD-1 inhibitors in non-small cell lung cancer by modulating gut microbiota and promoting anti-tumor immune responses. The findings suggest a potential new strategy to overcome immunotherapy resistance through gut microbiota-tumor immune crosstalk.

papers.title: Sparse time-varying log-ratios for longitudinal high-throughput sequencing data.
papers.abstract: High-throughput, longitudinal omics data, such as metabolomics or microbiome profiles, present analytical challenges owing to their compositional nature and irregular observation times. Although existing approaches can address compositional or temporal aspects separately, very few are tailored to capture both properties simultaneously in a high-dimensional setting. We introduce LCoDaCoRe as a supervised learning method to identify sparse time-varying log-ratio features from longitudinal, compositional data. The proposed approach integrates functional data analysis and continuous relaxation to
papers.summary: This paper introduces LCoDaCoRe, a new supervised learning method designed to identify sparse, time-varying log-ratio features from longitudinal, compositional omics data (like metabolomics or microbiome profiles). It addresses the combined challenges of compositional data structure and irregular observation times, which few existing methods handle simultaneously. The approach integrates functional data analysis with continuous relaxation to achieve this.

papers.title: Oral health status and subgingival microbiota in children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to compare the oral and dental health status and the oral microbiota of patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) with those of healthy children.
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
papers.summary: This study compared oral health and subgingival microbiota between children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) and healthy controls, finding differences in microbial composition associated with JIA. The results suggest that JIA may influence the oral microbiome, potentially impacting dental health management in these patients.

papers.title: Improving DirectLiNGAM for high-dimensional microbiome data: roots screening and eBIC based model selection.
papers.abstract: Identifying causal relationships from observational data is a central challenge in gut microbiome research, where complex, multivariate interactions shape host health and disease. These data are typically high-dimensional and sample-limited, creating substantial obstacles for causal discovery and motivating the development of methods tailored to this regime. In this study, we address this challenge by focusing on DirectLiNGAM and introducing two complementary methodological improvements designed to facilitate its practical application in microbiome data. Specifically, we propose two extensions
papers.summary: This study improves the DirectLiNGAM causal discovery method to better handle high-dimensional, sample-limited microbiome data by introducing two extensions: a roots screening step and an eBIC-based model selection procedure. These enhancements make the method more practical for identifying causal relationships in complex gut microbiome datasets.

papers.title: The Plastic Within: Micro- and Nanoplastics in Human Tissues and the Nutritional Context for Exposure Mitigation.
papers.abstract: Microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs) are increasingly detected in human tissues, prompting concern about potential biological effects. Yet, for most outcomes, the literature remains dominated by detection studies and preclinical toxicology, with limited human dose-response data.
papers.summary: This review discusses the growing evidence of micro- and nanoplastics found in human tissues and the potential health risks, while noting that most current research focuses on detection and animal studies rather than human dose-response data. It also explores nutritional strategies that might help reduce exposure or mitigate harm.

papers.title: Oral microbiome dynamics across periodontitis severity stratified by type 2 diabetes status.
papers.abstract: The oral microbiome serves as an effector of bidirectional promotion between periodontitis and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). However, the association between diabetes status and oral microbiota alterations and whether these patterns explained increased periodontitis severity remains unclearly illuminated.
papers.summary: This study examines how the oral microbiome changes with periodontitis severity in people with and without type 2 diabetes. The findings reveal distinct microbial patterns associated with diabetes that may explain increased periodontitis severity in diabetic patients.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence-driven phage therapy in veterinary medicine: an adaptive One Health strategy to mitigate antimicrobial resistance in livestock systems.
papers.abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in animal production systems is a major structural driver of the global resistance crisis. Food-producing animals account for the majority of global antimicrobial consumption, generating sustained selective pressure across livestock, environmental, and zoonotic bacterial reservoirs. Intensive poultry, swine, cattle, and aquaculture systems amplify pathogen transmission and accelerate resistance emergence. Bacteriophage therapy offers a species-specific, microbiome-preserving alternative to conventional antibiotics; however, large-scale veterinary implementation h
papers.summary: This review proposes using artificial intelligence to optimize bacteriophage therapy in livestock as a precision alternative to antibiotics, aiming to reduce antimicrobial resistance across animal, environmental, and human health. AI can accelerate phage selection, predict bacterial targets, and design therapeutic cocktails, making phage therapy more scalable for veterinary use. The approach aligns with a One Health strategy to combat the global resistance crisis.

papers.title: Compartment-Specific Variation in Bacterial Microbiome and Polyphyllin Profiles in Paris polyphylla.
papers.abstract: Paris polyphylla (P. polyphylla) is a valuable traditional Chinese medicinal plant, yet the spatial distribution of its compartment-specific bacterial microbiomes and their correlative relationships with bioactive polyphyllins remain poorly characterized. Here, we combined 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing, metabolite analysis, and bioinformatics to investigate the distribution patterns of bacterial communities and polyphyllins across bulk soil (BS), rhizosphere soil (RS), root endospheres (REs), stem endospheres (SEs), and leaf endospheres (LEs) of P. polyphylla. A spot inoculation assay was furth
papers.summary: This study characterizes the bacterial microbiomes and polyphyllin profiles across different compartments (soil, root, stem, leaf) of the medicinal plant Paris polyphylla, revealing distinct spatial patterns and correlations. The findings provide insights into how compartment-specific bacterial communities may influence the accumulation of bioactive compounds in this traditional Chinese medicine.
